infobox.value: ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Região Sudeste do Brasil*, pronounced [[ʁeʒiˈɐ̃w suˈdɛstʃi du bɾaˈziw]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is composed of the states of [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo"), [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"), and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_State "São Paulo State"). It is the richest region of the country, responsible for approximately 53% of the Brazilian [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (2022),[[2]](#cite_note-ibge_PIB_2022-2) as São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and Minas Gerais are the three richest states of Brazil, the top three Brazilian states in terms of GDP. The Southeast of Brazil also has the highest [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") among all Brazilian regions.

first_paragraph: The **Southeast Region of Brazil** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Região Sudeste do Brasil*, pronounced [[ʁeʒiˈɐ̃w suˈdɛstʃi du bɾaˈziw]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is composed of the states of [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo"), [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"), and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_State "São Paulo State"). It is the richest region of the country, responsible for approximately 53% of the Brazilian [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (2022),[[2]](#cite_note-ibge_PIB_2022-2) as São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and Minas Gerais are the three richest states of Brazil, the top three Brazilian states in terms of GDP. The Southeast of Brazil also has the highest [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") among all Brazilian regions.

The **Southeast Region of Brazil** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Região Sudeste do Brasil*, pronounced [[ʁeʒiˈɐ̃w suˈdɛstʃi du bɾaˈziw]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is composed of the states of [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo"), [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"), and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_State "São Paulo State"). It is the richest region of the country, responsible for approximately 53% of the Brazilian [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (2022),[[2]](#cite_note-ibge_PIB_2022-2) as São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and Minas Gerais are the three richest states of Brazil, the top three Brazilian states in terms of GDP. The Southeast of Brazil also has the highest [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") among all Brazilian regions.

The Southeast region accounts for about 44% of Brazil's total population, leading the country in population, urban population, population density, vehicles, industries, universities, airports, ports, highways, hospitals, schools, houses and many other areas.

### São Paulo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: São Paulo")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Ip%C3%AAs_lil%C3%A1s_na_coronel_quirino_cambu%C3%AD_001.jpg/250px-Ip%C3%AAs_lil%C3%A1s_na_coronel_quirino_cambu%C3%AD_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ip%C3%AAs_lil%C3%A1s_na_coronel_quirino_cambu%C3%AD_001.jpg)

[Ipê](/wiki/Ip%C3%AA "Ipê") in [Campinas](/wiki/Campinas "Campinas").

Heart of the largest continued remnant of the Brazilian [Atlantic Forest](/wiki/Atlantic_Forest "Atlantic Forest"), the [Ribeira Valley](/wiki/Ribeira_Valley "Ribeira Valley") is a Natural Heritage of Humanity, granted heritage as a Biosphere Reserve by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"). One of the biggest attractions is the biologic and ecosystems diversity, where approximately 400 species of [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird"), amphibians, reptiles and [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") live. The [Alto Ribeira Tourist State Park](/wiki/Alto_Ribeira_Tourist_State_Park "Alto Ribeira Tourist State Park") (PETAR) is paradise for ecotourists, for its enormous diversity in geologic formations, among [grottos](/wiki/Grotto "Grotto") and [caves](/wiki/Cave "Cave"), [rivers](/wiki/River "River") and [waterfalls](/wiki/Waterfall "Waterfall"). There are currently 454 caves registered by the Brazilian Society of Speleology (SBE) in the State of São Paulo, all at the Ribeira Valley. The 280 caves located at PETAR represent the biggest concentration of caves in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil").

The landscape of the State is marked by [mountains](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain"), [valleys](/wiki/Valley "Valley") and [caverns](/wiki/Cave "Cave"). In the Serra do Cipó, Sete Lagoas, Cordisburgo and Lagoa Santa, the [caves](/wiki/Cave "Cave") and [waterfalls](/wiki/Waterfall "Waterfall"). Minas Gerais is the source of some of the biggest rivers in Brazil, most notably the [São Francisco](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Francisco_River "São Francisco River"), the [Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_River "Paraná River") and to a lesser extent, the [Rio Doce](/wiki/Doce_River "Doce River"). The state also holds many hydroelectric power plants, including [Furnas dam](/wiki/Furnas_Dam "Furnas Dam"). Some of the highest peaks in Brazil are in the mountain ranges in the southern part of the state, such as Serra da Mantiqueira and Serra do Cervo, which mark the border between Minas and its neighbors [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") and [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). The most notable one is the [Pico da Bandeira](/wiki/Pico_da_Bandeira "Pico da Bandeira"), the third highest mountain in Brazil at 2890 m, standing on the border with Espírito Santo state. The state also has huge reserves of [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") and sizeable reserves of [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") and [gemstones](/wiki/Gemstones "Gemstones"), including [emerald](/wiki/Emerald "Emerald"), [topaz](/wiki/Topaz "Topaz") and [aquamarine](/wiki/Beryl#Aquamarine_and_maxixe "Beryl") mines.

### Rio de Janeiro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Rio de Janeiro")]

The state is part of the [Mata Atlântica](/wiki/Mata_Atl%C3%A2ntica "Mata Atlântica") [biome](/wiki/Biome "Biome"), and its topography comprises both [mountains](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") and plains, located between the [Mantiqueira Mountains](/wiki/Mantiqueira_Mountains "Mantiqueira Mountains") and the Atlantic Ocean. Its coast is carved by the bays of [Guanabara](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay "Guanabara Bay"), Sepetiba and Ilha Grande.
There are prominent [slopes](/wiki/Slope "Slope") near the ocean, also featuring diverse environments, such as [restinga vegetation](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_restingas "Atlantic Coast restingas"), [bays](/wiki/Bay "Bay"), [lagoons](/wiki/Lagoon "Lagoon") and [tropical forests](/wiki/Tropical_forest "Tropical forest").
Rio de Janeiro is the smallest state in the Southeast [macroregion](/wiki/Macroregion_(Brazil) "Macroregion (Brazil)") and one of the smallest in Brazil. It has, however, the third longest coastline in the country (second only to [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia")'s and [Maranhão](/wiki/Maranh%C3%A3o "Maranhão")'s), extending 635 kilometers.

### Espírito Santo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Espírito Santo")]

With a 46.180 square kilometers, it is about the size of Estonia, or half the size of Portugal, and has a variety of habitats including coastal plains, lakes, mountain forest, mangroves and many others.
The main river in the state is the [Doce](/wiki/Doce_River "Doce River"). Other important river basins include the Santa Maria River Basin which is the northern branch of rivers which join the sea at Vitoria, and Jucu River Basin which flows into the sea at roughly the same place, but corresponds to the southern branch (which seems to come out of Vitoria). Espírito Santo's climate is [tropical](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") along the coast, with dry winters and rainy summers. North of [Doce River](/wiki/Doce_River "Doce River") it's generally drier and also hot. In the mountainous regions in the south and south west of the state, the tropical climate is strongly influenced by [altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude"), and the average temperatures are colder.
The state can be divided into two areas: the low lying coastline and the highland area known as *Serra* (where one can find the 2.890 m [Pico da Bandeira](/wiki/Pico_da_Bandeira "Pico da Bandeira") mountain), which is part of the larger [Serra do Caparaó](/wiki/Serra_do_Capara%C3%B3 "Serra do Caparaó"), the Caparaó Mountain Range. In the map to the right it is in the gray area in the extreme southwest of the state, and is shared with Minas Gerais.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Coffee_Plantation.jpg/250px-Coffee_Plantation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coffee_Plantation.jpg)

Coffee plantation in [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Laranjal_Cutrale_050806_REFON_3.jpg/250px-Laranjal_Cutrale_050806_REFON_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laranjal_Cutrale_050806_REFON_3.jpg)

Orange plantation in São Paulo.

The main agricultural [products](/wiki/Product_(business) "Product (business)") grown are:

* [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee") (85% of the country's production, which is the world's largest producer);
* [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane") (65% of the country's production, which is the world's largest producer);
* [orange](/wiki/Orange_(fruit) "Orange (fruit)") (85% of the country's production, which is the world's largest producer);
* [lemon](/wiki/Lemon "Lemon") (80% of the country's production, which is the fifth largest producer in the world);
* [peanut](/wiki/Peanut "Peanut") (90% of the country's production, which is the 14th largest producer in the world);
* [banana](/wiki/Banana "Banana") (35% of the country's production, which is the seventh largest producer in the world);
* [sorghum](/wiki/Sorghum "Sorghum") (30% of national production, the country being the seventh largest producer);
* [persimmon](/wiki/Persimmon "Persimmon") (70% of national production, the country being the 6th largest producer in the world);
* [tomato](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato") (44% of national production, the country being the 10th largest producer);
* [tangerine](/wiki/Tangerine "Tangerine") (60% of national production, the country being the 6th largest producer in the world);
* [papaya](/wiki/Papaya "Papaya") (40% of national production, the country being the 2nd largest producer in the world);
* [pineapple](/wiki/Pineapple "Pineapple") (27% of national production, the country being the 3rd world producer);
* [cassava](/wiki/Cassava "Cassava") (13% of national production, the country being the 5th world producer).

The region still produces considerable amounts of:

* [bean](/wiki/Bean "Bean") (more than 20% of national production, the country being the 3rd world producer);
* [soy](/wiki/Soy "Soy") (7% of the country's production, which is the largest producer in the world);
* [potato](/wiki/Potato "Potato"), [carrot](/wiki/Carrot "Carrot") and [strawberry](/wiki/Strawberry "Strawberry") (more than 50% of the country's production).[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Livestock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Livestock")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Vaca_leite.JPG/250px-Vaca_leite.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vaca_leite.JPG)

Milk extraction in Minas Gerais.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Visita_Microbacia_de_Jo%C3%A3o_Ramalho_-_Jo%C3%A3o_Ramalho_07-02-2015.jpg/250px-Visita_Microbacia_de_Jo%C3%A3o_Ramalho_-_Jo%C3%A3o_Ramalho_07-02-2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Visita_Microbacia_de_Jo%C3%A3o_Ramalho_-_Jo%C3%A3o_Ramalho_07-02-2015.jpg)

Cattle in São Paulo

The [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock") also has great prominence in the region.

The Southeast is the second national producer of [milk](/wiki/Milk "Milk"), with 34.2%, slightly losing compared to the South Region, which produces 35.7%. The southeast has the largest herd of cows milked: 30.4% of the total of 17.1 million existing in Brazil. The highest productivity, however, is that of the Southern Region, with an average of 3,284 liters per cow per year, which is why it has led the milk production ranking since 2015. Minas Gerais is the main milk-producing state in Brazil with the largest it is also effective for milked cows, responsible for 26.6% of production and 20.0% of all animals in milk. The municipality of Patos de Minas was the second largest producer in 2017, with 191.3 million liters of milk.

Poultry farming and egg production are the most important in the country. Flocks of poultry, according to IBGE, include chickens, roosters, hens and chicks. In 2018, the South region, with an emphasis on the creation of chickens for slaughter, was responsible for almost half of the Brazilian total (46.9%). Paraná only represented 26.2%. However, the situation is reversed when it comes to chickens. The first region in the ranking was the Southeast, with 38.9% of the country's total capital. A total of 246.9 million hens was estimated for 2018. The state of São Paulo was responsible for 21.9%. The national production of chicken eggs was 4.4 billion dozen in 2018. The Southeast region was responsible for 43.8% of the total produced. The state of São Paulo was the largest national producer (25.6%), with a total of 16.8 million birds. The Southeast is responsible for 64%, highlighting São Paulo (24.6%) and Espírito Santo (21.0%).

### Mining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Mining")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Itabira_MG_Brasil_-_Mina_de_Ferro_da_Vale_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Itabira_MG_Brasil_-_Mina_de_Ferro_da_Vale_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Itabira_MG_Brasil_-_Mina_de_Ferro_da_Vale_-_panoramio.jpg)

Iron mine in Minas Gerais.

In mineral production, in 2017, Minas Gerais was the country's largest producer of [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") (277 million tons worth R $37.2 billion), [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") (29.3 tons for a value of R $3.6 billion), [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") (400 thousand tons for a value of R $351 million) and [niobium](/wiki/Niobium "Niobium") (in the form of hydrochloride) (131 thousand tons for a value of R $254 million). In addition, Minas was the second largest producer of [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminum "Aluminum") ([bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite")) (1.47 million tons at a value of R $105 million), third of [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese") (296 thousand tons to a value of R $32 million) and 5th of [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin") (206 tons worth R $4.7 million). Minas Gerais had 47.19% of the value of the production of minerals marketed in Brazil (first place), with R $41.7 billion.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The state has the largest production of various gemstones in the country. In [aquamarine](/wiki/Aquamarine_(gemstone) "Aquamarine (gemstone)"), Minas Gerais produces the most valuable stones in the world. In [diamond](/wiki/Diamond "Diamond"), Brazil was the world's largest producer of diamonds from 1730 to 1870, mining occurred for the first time in the [Serra da Canastra](/wiki/Serra_da_Canastra "Serra da Canastra"), region of [Diamantina](/wiki/Diamantina,_Minas_Gerais "Diamantina, Minas Gerais"), even lowering the price of stone in everyone due to excess production. Minas Gerais continues to extract diamonds, in addition to having larger or smaller scale productions of [agate](/wiki/Agate "Agate"), [emerald](/wiki/Emerald "Emerald"), [garnet](/wiki/Garnet "Garnet"), [jasper](/wiki/Jasper "Jasper"), [amethyst](/wiki/Amethyst "Amethyst"), and [sapphire](/wiki/Sapphire "Sapphire"). [Topaz](/wiki/Topaz "Topaz") and [tourmaline](/wiki/Tourmaline "Tourmaline") stand out. Imperial Topaz, a valued variety of topaz, is only found in [Ouro Preto](/wiki/Ouro_Preto "Ouro Preto"). Brazil is the world's leading producer of topaz. It is also one of the world's largest tourmaline producers.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the Southeast region, the industrialization of the country began, making the transformation industry the main source of income and work in its states. The state of São Paulo became the largest industrial park in South America.

The Southeast Region has 53% of the industrial GDP of Brazil.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The main industrial activities in the region are:

* [Iron and steel industry](/wiki/Iron_and_steel_industry "Iron and steel industry") and [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy"): It's in this region that the first industry of this type is located, the [Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional](/wiki/Companhia_Sider%C3%BArgica_Nacional "Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional"), in the city of [Volta Redonda](/wiki/Volta_Redonda "Volta Redonda"), due to its proximity to a large area of iron mining, the so-called "iron quadrilateral", in the state of Minas Gerais. The [Usiminas](/wiki/Usiminas "Usiminas"), in [Ipatinga](/wiki/Ipatinga "Ipatinga"), which today is the largest crude steel producer in the country, and the Companhia Siderúrgica de Tubarão, in Vitória, Espírito Santo, are also installed in the region. The Southeast Region produces around 90% of the country's steel.

In Brazil, the automobile sector represents around 22% of industrial GDP. The Greater ABC Region, in São Paulo, is the first center and the largest automobile center in Brazil. When the country's manufacturing was practically restricted to ABC, the State represented 74.8% of Brazilian production in 1990. In 2017, this index decreased to 46.6%, and in 2019, to 40.1%, due to a phenomenon of internalization of the production of vehicles in Brazil, driven by factors such as unions, which made excessive labor and payroll charges, discouraged investment and favored the search for new cities. The development of the ABC cities helped curb appeal, due to rising real estate costs and higher density of residential areas. Sul Fluminense (Rio de Janeiro) was already the second largest pole in 2017, but in 2019 it fell to fourth place, behind Paraná (15%) and Minas Gerais (10.7%). In the Southeast there are factories of GM, Volkswagen, Fiat, Ford, Honda, Toyota, Mitsubishi, Nissan, Hyundai, Mercedes-Benz, Land Rover, Citroen / Peugeot, Scania and Iveco.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In the production of [tractors](/wiki/Tractors "Tractors"), in 2017, the main manufacturers in Brazil were [John Deere](/wiki/John_Deere "John Deere"), [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland_Agriculture "New Holland Agriculture"), [Massey Ferguson](/wiki/Massey_Ferguson "Massey Ferguson"), [Valtra](/wiki/Valtra "Valtra"), [Case IH](/wiki/Case_IH "Case IH") and the Brazilian [Agrale](/wiki/Agrale "Agrale"). They all have factories in the southeast, basically in São Paulo.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

* [Petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"): Almost all of Brazil's oil and natural gas is extracted in the Southeast, mainly in Rio de Janeiro, but also in Espírito Santo and São Paulo. Most of the oil refining is also carried out in the region. Rio de Janeiro presents great importance in oil prospecting, which São Paulo has a great importance in the refining activity, being located in that state the main refineries of the country, among them, the REPLAN, the main of the country. In addition to oil, there is the extraction of natural gas from the Santos maritime basin and, until a few years ago, there was the extraction of bitumen in the Paraíba river valley.
* High technology: The so-called "Brazilian silicon valley" is located in that region, made up of the cities of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), [São José dos Campos](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jos%C3%A9_dos_Campos "São José dos Campos"), [São Carlos](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Carlos,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Carlos, São Paulo") and [Campinas](/wiki/Campinas "Campinas"). These four cities concentrate industries of [computing](/wiki/Computing "Computing"), [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications"), [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") and other activities involving high technology; in addition to having important research centers and important [universities](/wiki/University "University"), such as the "Technological Institute of Aeronautics" - ITA, in São José dos Campos. Brazil has two large production centers for electronic products, located in the Metropolitan Region of Campinas, in the State of São Paulo, and in the Manaus Free Trade Zone, in the State of Amazonas. There are large internationally renowned technology companies, as well as part of the industries that participate in their supply chain. The country also has other smaller centers, such as the municipalities of [São José dos Campos](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jos%C3%A9_dos_Campos "São José dos Campos") and [São Carlos](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Carlos "São Carlos"), in the state of São Paulo. In Campinas there are industrial units of groups such as [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric"), [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung"), [HP](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard") and [Foxconn](/wiki/Foxconn "Foxconn"), a manufacturer of [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") and [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell"). São José dos Campos, focuses on the aviation industry. This is where the headquarters of [Embraer](/wiki/Embraer "Embraer"), a Brazilian company that is the third largest aircraft manufacturer in the world, after Boeing and Airbus, is located. In the production of cell phones and other electronic products, Samsung produces in [Campinas](/wiki/Campinas "Campinas"); LG produces in [Taubaté](/wiki/Taubat%C3%A9 "Taubaté"); Flextronics, which produces Motorola cell phones, produces in [Jaguariúna](/wiki/Jaguari%C3%BAna "Jaguariúna"); and Semp-TCL produces in [Cajamar](/wiki/Cajamar,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Cajamar, São Paulo"). In Santa Rita do Sapucaí, Minas Gerais, 8 thousand jobs are linked to the sector, with more than 120 companies. Most produce equipment for the telecommunications industry, such as decoders, including those used in the transmission of the digital television system.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 2011, Brazil had the sixth largest [chemical industry](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry") in the world, with net sales of $157 billion, or 3.1% of world sales. At that time, there were 973 factories of chemical products for industrial use. They are concentrated in the Southeast Region, mainly in São Paulo. In 2018, the Brazilian chemical sector was the eighth largest in the world, representing 10% of the national industrial GDP and 2.5% of the total GDP. In 2020, imports will occupy 43% of domestic demand for chemical products. Since 2008, the average use of capacity in the Brazilian chemical industry has been at a level considered low, ranging between 70 and 83%.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In [pharmaceutical Industry](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_Industry "Pharmaceutical Industry"), most of the companies in Brazil have been established in São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro for a long time. In 2019, the situation was that, due to the tax advantages offered in states such as Pernambuco, Goiás and Minas Gerais, companies were leaving RJ and SP and going to these states. In 2017, Brazil was considered the sixth largest pharmaceutical market in the world. Drug sales in pharmacies reached around R $57 billion (US$17.79 billion) in the country. The pharmaceutical market in Brazil had 241 regulated and authorized laboratories for the sale of medicines. Of these, the majority (60%) have national capital. Multinational companies had approximately 52.44% of the market, with 34.75% in commercialized packaging. Brazilian laboratories represent 47.56% of the market in sales and 65.25% in cases sold. In the distribution of drug sales by state, São Paulo ranked first: São Paulo's pharmaceutical industry had a turnover of R $53.3 billion, 76.8% of total sales throughout the country. The companies that benefited the most from the sale of drugs in the country in 2015 were [EMS](/wiki/EMS_(pharmaceuticals) "EMS (pharmaceuticals)"), [Hypermarcas](/wiki/Hypermarcas "Hypermarcas") (NeoQuímica), [Sanofi](/wiki/Sanofi "Sanofi") (Medley), [Novartis](/wiki/Novartis "Novartis"), [Aché](/wiki/Ach%C3%A9 "Aché"), [Eurofarma](/wiki/Eurofarma "Eurofarma"), [Takeda](/wiki/Takeda_Pharmaceutical_Company "Takeda Pharmaceutical Company"), [Bayer](/wiki/Bayer "Bayer"), [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer") and [GSK](/wiki/GlaxoSmithKline "GlaxoSmithKline").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In [food industry](/wiki/Food_industry "Food industry"), in 2019, Brazil was the second largest exporter of processed foods in the world, with a value of US$34.1 billion in exports. The revenue of the Brazilian food and beverage industry in 2019 was R $699.9 billion, 9.7% of the country's gross domestic product. In 2015, the food and beverage industrial sector in Brazil comprised 34,800 companies (not counting bakeries), the vast majority of which were small. These companies employed more than 1,600,000 workers, making the food and beverage industry the largest employer in manufacturing. There are around 570 large companies in Brazil, which account for a good part of the industry's total income. São Paulo created companies such as: [Yoki](/w/index.php?title=Yoki_(company)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yoki (company) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yoki "pt:Yoki")], [Vigor](/wiki/Vigor_S.A. "Vigor S.A."), [Minerva Foods](/wiki/Minerva_Foods "Minerva Foods"), [Bauducco](/w/index.php?title=Bauducco&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bauducco (page does not exist)"), [Santa Helena](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Helena_(company)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Santa Helena (company) (page does not exist)"), [Marilan](/w/index.php?title=Marilan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marilan (page does not exist)"), [Ceratti](/w/index.php?title=Ceratti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ceratti (page does not exist)"), [Fugini](/w/index.php?title=Fugini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fugini (page does not exist)"), [Chocolates Pan](/w/index.php?title=Chocolates_Pan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chocolates Pan (page does not exist)"), [Embaré](/w/index.php?title=Embar%C3%A9&action=edit&redlink=1 "Embaré (page does not exist)"), among others. Minas Gerais created food companies of national importance such as [Itambé](/wiki/Itamb%C3%A9 "Itambé") and [Pif Paf Alimentos](/w/index.php?title=Pif_Paf_Alimentos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pif Paf Alimentos (page does not exist)"). Espírito Santo created [Chocolates Garoto](/wiki/Chocolates_Garoto "Chocolates Garoto") and Rio de Janeiro created [Piraquê](/wiki/Piraqu%C3%AA "Piraquê") and [Granfino](/w/index.php?title=Granfino&action=edit&redlink=1 "Granfino (page does not exist)").[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In the [Footwear industry](/wiki/Footwear_industry "Footwear industry"), in 2019 Brazil produced 972 million pairs. Exports were around 10%, reaching almost 125 million pairs. Brazil ranks fourth among world producers, behind China, India and Vietnam, and 11th among the largest exporters. Of the pairs produced, 49% were made of plastic or rubber, 28.8% were made of synthetic laminate, and only 17.7% were made of leather. The largest pole in Brazil is in Rio Grande do Sul, but São Paulo has important shoe centers, such as the one in the city of [Franca](/wiki/Franca "Franca"), specialized in men's footwear, in the city of [Jaú](/wiki/Ja%C3%BA "Jaú"), specialized in women's footwear and in the city of [Birigui](/wiki/Birigui "Birigui"), specialized in footwear. children. Jaú, Franca and Birigui represent 92% of footwear production in the state of São Paulo. Birigui has 350 companies, which generate around 13 thousand jobs, producing 45.9 million pairs per year. 52% of children's shoes in the country are produced in this city. From Birigui came most of the most famous children's shoe factories in the country. Jaú has 150 factories that produce around 130 thousand pairs of cheap women's shoes per day. The footwear sector in Franca has around 550 companies and employs about 20,000 employees. Most of the most famous men's shoe factories in the country come from São Paulo. Minas Gerais has a pole specialized in cheap shoes and shoes in [Nova Serrana](/wiki/Nova_Serrana "Nova Serrana"). The city has around 830 industries, which in 2017 produced around 110 million pairs. However, overall, the Brazilian industry has been struggling to compete with Chinese footwear, which is priced unbeatable due to the difference in tax collection from one country to another, in addition to the absence of heavy Brazilian labor taxes in China, and the Brazilian businessman has had to invest in value-added products, combining quality and design, in order to survive.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In [textile industry](/wiki/Textile_industry "Textile industry"), Brazil, despite being among the 5 largest producers in the world in 2013, and being representative in the consumption of textiles and clothing, has very little insertion in world trade. In 2015, Brazilian imports ranked 25th (US$5.5 billion). And in exports, it was only 40th in the world ranking. Brazil's participation in the world trade of textiles and clothing is only 0.3%, due to the difficulty of competing in price with producers in India and mainly in China. The gross value of production, which includes the consumption of intermediate goods and services, of the Brazilian textile industry corresponded to almost R $40 billion in 2015, 1.6% of the gross value of industrial production in Brazil. São Paulo (37.4%) is the largest producer. The main productive areas of São Paulo are the Metropolitan Region of São Paulo and Campinas. Minas Gerais has 8.51% (third largest production in the country).[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In home appliance industry, sales were 12.9 million units in 2017. The sector had its sales peak in 2012, with 18.9 million units. The brands that sold the most were Brastemp, Electrolux, Consul and Philips. [Brastemp](/w/index.php?title=Brastemp&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brastemp (page does not exist)") is originally from [São Bernardo do Campo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Bernardo_do_Campo "São Bernardo do Campo"). São Paulo was also the place where [Metalfrio](/w/index.php?title=Metalfrio&action=edit&redlink=1 "Metalfrio (page does not exist)") was founded.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### International Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: International Airports")]

* [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Guarulhos5.jpg/250px-Guarulhos5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guarulhos5.jpg)

[São Paulo-Guarulhos International Airport (GRU)](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo-Guarulhos_International_Airport "São Paulo-Guarulhos International Airport").

[São Paulo-Guarulhos International Airport](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo-Guarulhos_International_Airport "São Paulo-Guarulhos International Airport") connects [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") to 28 countries with nearly 100,000 daily visitors. With capacity to serve 15 million passengers a year, in two terminals, the airport currently handles 12 million users. Construction of a third passenger terminal is pending, to raise yearly capacity to 29 million [passengers](/wiki/Passengers "Passengers"). The project, in the tendering phase, is part of the airport's master plan and will get under way shortly. São Paulo International Airport is also one of the main air cargo hubs in Brazil. The roughly 100 flights a day carry everything from fruits grown in the [São Francisco Valley](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Francisco_Valley&action=edit&redlink=1 "São Francisco Valley (page does not exist)") to the most sophisticated medications created by science. The [airport](/wiki/Airport "Airport")'s cargo terminal is [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America")'s largest and stands behind only [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City")'s in all of [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). In 2003, over 75,000 metric tons of freight passed through the [terminal](/wiki/Container_terminal "Container terminal").

* [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro")

Since August 2004, with the transfer of many flights from Santos-Dumont Airport, Rio de Janeiro International Airport has returned to being the main doorway to [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). According to data from the official Brazilian travel bureau, Embratur, nearly 40% of foreign tourists who visit Brazil choose Rio as their gateway, meaning Galeão Airport. Besides linking Rio to the rest of Brazil with domestic flights, Galeão has connections to more than 18 countries. It can handle up to 15 million users a year in two passenger terminals. Located only 20 kilometers from downtown Rio, the international airport is served by several quick access routes, such as the Linha Vermelha and Linha Amarela freeways and Avenida Brasil, thus conveniently serving residents of the city's southern, northern and western zones. There are special shuttle buses linking Galeão to Santos-Dumont, and bus and taxi service to the rest of the city. The airport complex also has Brazil's longest runway at 4.240 meters, and one of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America")'s largest and best equipped cargo logistics terminals.

* [Belo Horizonte](/wiki/Belo_Horizonte "Belo Horizonte")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Aeroporto_de_Confins.jpg/250px-Aeroporto_de_Confins.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aeroporto_de_Confins.jpg)

The [Confins International Airport](/wiki/Confins_International_Airport "Confins International Airport") in the main hub in Minas Gerais.

Tancredo Neves/[Confins International Airport](/wiki/Confins_International_Airport "Confins International Airport") is located in the municipalities of Lagoa Santa and Confins, 38 km from Belo Horizonte, and was opened in January 1984. It was planned from the start for future expansion in steps to meet growing demand. The first step was undertaken with careful concern for the environment, including monitoring by specialized consultants, since the region has a rich archeological heritage. The airport's location is attested to by the fact it has one of the lowest rates of shutdown for bad weather in the country. Confins is certified by the ISO 9001 standard, covering ten processes in the administrative, operational, safety/security and maintenance areas. Starting in March 2005, Confins Airport will receive flights that currently use [Pampulha Airport](/wiki/Pampulha_Airport "Pampulha Airport").

### National Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: National Airport")]

* [Vitória](/wiki/Vit%C3%B3ria,_Brazil "Vitória, Brazil")

[Eurico de Aguiar Salles Airport](/wiki/Eurico_de_Aguiar_Salles_Airport "Eurico de Aguiar Salles Airport") is located on a land plot of just over 5.2 million square meters. Since construction of its first step, finished in 1946, Vitória Airport has undergone several expansions and modernizations, but current demand has surpassed its capacity of 560 thousand passengers a year. The recent construction of new aircraft parking boxes on the aprons has improved the airport's operational efficiency.
In 2003 more than 1.2 million passengers used the airport, and in 2004 this rose to some 1.25 million. Vitória is one of the 32 airports in the Infraero network that has a cargo terminal. In May 1999 the first direct international freight connection to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") ([Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami")) began operating to Vitória, facilitating imports to the state of [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo"). Today there are five of such flights a week.

### Highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southeast_Region,_Brazil&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Highways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Dom-pedro-km145.jpg/250px-Dom-pedro-km145.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dom-pedro-km145.jpg)

Dom Pedro Highway, part of [Campinas](/wiki/Campinas "Campinas") Beltway. Photo by Renato M.E. Sabbatini.

The Southeast Brazilian region is highly covered by [Paved roads](/wiki/Paved_road "Paved road") – due to the policy in the Brazil's economy in the 1960s (automobilization of the country) – which led to the car's manufacturing to be a major industry not only in the region but in Brazil as well.

The Southeast's highways are generally in good or very good conditions – some exceptions are made to the southern parts of the [BR-101](/wiki/BR-101 "BR-101").

[Railways](/wiki/Railway "Railway") are present as well (and also, the region is more covered than any other region), but mainly for freight transport – soy beans and iron ore, mainly, from the farms and mines to the seaports.

Also, a very complicated hydrocanal system is present in the region, with ten major dams.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/IgrejaPampulha.jpg/250px-IgrejaPampulha.jpg)](/wiki/File:IgrejaPampulha.jpg)

The Pampulha Church in Belo Horizonte is one of the most expressive symbols of the Brazilian Modern Architecture.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Centro_SP2.jpg/250px-Centro_SP2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Centro_SP2.jpg)

[São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") is the largest Brazilian city.

Health care and education are of major concern in the larger cities, but in smaller ones, the population are generally well-looked after.
